questions.text: Statement: I. The school authority has asked the FSC students to attend special classes to be conducted on Sundays. II. The parents of the FSC students have withdrawn their wards from attending private tuitions conducted on Sundays.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Statement I is the cause and statement II is its effect.
options.explanation: The two statements are unrelated and independent of each other. There is no clear cause-and-effect relationship between them based on the information provided. Statement I: "The school authority has asked the FSC students to attend special classes to be conducted on Sundays." This statement indicates that the school authority has scheduled special classes for FSC students on Sundays. It does not provide any information about why these special classes are being conducted or how it relates to private tuitions. Statement II: "The parents of the FSC students have withdrawn their wards from attending private tuitions conducted on Sundays." This statement states that the parents of FSC students have decided to stop sending their children to private tuitions that were previously conducted on Sundays. However, it does not mention anything about the reason for this decision or whether it is related to the special classes organized by the school. Without additional information or context, we cannot determine any direct connection between the school's special classes and the parents' decision to withdraw their children from private tuitions on Sundays. The two statements might be independent events or have separate reasons behind them.

options.text: Statement II is the cause and statement I is its effect
Both the statements I and II are independent causes
Both the statements I and II are effects of some common cause

questions.explanation: The reason why parents have withdrawn their wards from attending private tuitions on Sunday is that the students had to attend special classes conducted by the school on Sunday. If there were no classes conducted by the school on Sunday then the parents wouldn’t have had to make their kids leave their Sunday tuitions.

questions.text: Find the odd one.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.explanation: Mother, sister and aunt are all related to a person by blood hence they fall in the same category. However, a person has no blood relation with the maid, a female domestic servant, thus she is the odd one out/different one.

questions.text: Statement: Should children be prevented completely from watching television? Arguments: (I) No. We get vital information regarding education through television. (II) Yes. It hampers the study of children. (Ill) Yes. Young children are misguided by certain programmes featuring violence.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Only I, II and Ill are strong
options.explanation: Both arguments (I) and (III) are strong. Argument (I) states that children should not be completely prevented from watching television because television provides vital information regarding education. This argument highlights the educational value of television and how it can be a valuable source of learning for children. Restricting television entirely could deprive them of educational content that can supplement their studies and knowledge. Argument (III) states that children should be prevented from watching television because certain programs featuring violence can misguide young children. This argument points out a potential negative impact of television on young minds, especially when they are exposed to violent or inappropriate content. Shielding children from such content can protect their mental and emotional well-being. Both arguments present valid points, and a balanced approach would be necessary in addressing the role of television in a child's life. It is essential to provide access to educational content while also ensuring that exposure to potentially harmful or unsuitable material is minimized or restricted. Parental guidance and responsible media choices can help strike the right balance in children's television viewing habits. Since argument (II) is not strong, this option is incorrect.

Clearly, television offers various educational programs which are of great practical value to students. So, it serves as a means (but it is not the 'only' means) to educate the masses. Thus, I hold strong while IV does not. Besides, the demerits of watching television, mentioned in II and III, may be done away with by allowing children to watch selected programs on television, according to a set schedule. So, neither II nor III holds strong.

options.text: Only I and III are strong
options.explanation: Both arguments (I) and (III) are strong. Argument (I) states that children should not be completely prevented from watching television because television provides vital information regarding education. This argument highlights the educational value of television and how it can be a valuable source of learning for children. Restricting television entirely could deprive them of educational content that can supplement their studies and knowledge. Argument (III) states that children should be prevented from watching television because certain programs featuring violence can misguide young children. This argument points out a potential negative impact of television on young minds, especially when they are exposed to violent or inappropriate content. Shielding children from such content can protect their mental and emotional well-being. Both arguments present valid points, and a balanced approach would be necessary in addressing the role of television in a child's life. It is essential to provide access to educational content while also ensuring that exposure to potentially harmful or unsuitable material is minimized or restricted. Parental guidance and responsible media choices can help strike the right balance in children's television viewing habits.

options.text: Only I and II are strong
options.explanation: Both arguments (I) and (III) are strong. Argument (I) states that children should not be completely prevented from watching television because television provides vital information regarding education. This argument highlights the educational value of television and how it can be a valuable source of learning for children. Restricting television entirely could deprive them of educational content that can supplement their studies and knowledge. Both arguments present valid points, and a balanced approach would be necessary in addressing the role of television in a child's life. It is essential to provide access to educational content while also ensuring that exposure to potentially harmful or unsuitable material is minimized or restricted. Parental guidance and responsible media choices can help strike the right balance in children's television viewing habits. Since arguments (I) and (III) are correct, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Both arguments (I) and (III) are strong.

questions.text: Statements: No cities are countries. No countries are villages. Conclusions: (I) Some countries are cities.(II) No villages are the city.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Option A (Only conclusion (I) follows):Conclusion (I) states, "Some countries are cities." However, the given statements do not provide any information about the relationship between countries and cities, so this conclusion cannot be logically derived from the statements.

Option B (Only conclusion (II) follows): Conclusion (II) states, "No villages are the city." This conclusion is not supported by the given statements. The statements only indicate that countries and villages are distinct, but they do not provide any information about cities and villages.

Option C (Both conclusions follow):Neither of the conclusions can be logically derived from the given statements. The statements only establish relationships between cities and countries and between countries and villages, but they do not establish any relationship between cities and villages. Therefore, both conclusions are invalid.

options.text: Both of the conclusions do not follow
options.explanation: The least possible Venn diagram for the given statements is as follows, Conclusion:(i)Some countries are city - It is not possible,hence false.(ii)No villages are city - It is possible but not definite,hence false.Hence,neither conclusion (i) nor conclusion (ii) follows.

questions.explanation: The least possible Venn diagram for the given statements is as follows, Conclusion:(i)Some countries are city - It is not possible,hence false.(ii)No villages are city - It is possible but not definite,hence false.Hence,neither conclusion (i) nor conclusion (ii) follows.

questions.text: Statement: Should admission to the professional degrees in Pakistan be given only on merit without any concession to any particular group of students?Arguments:(I) Yes. This will improve the quality of the professional institutes and degrees as they will be able to complete the degree successfully. (II) No. This will keep a large number of socially and economically backward students out of the reach of professional institutes and degrees.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because it ignores the potential benefits of providing concessions to certain groups of students. Merit-based admissions can indeed improve the quality of professional institutes, but it does not address the issue of ensuring access and opportunities for underprivileged students.

Option B is incorrect because it overlooks the importance of promoting inclusivity and diversity in professional institutes. Without concessions or affirmative action, socially and economically backward students may face significant barriers to accessing higher education, perpetuating inequalities.

options.text: Both arguments I and II are strong
options.explanation: Our country seeks to support educationally and economically backward classes for their overall growth and development.However due to merit based admissions we can assure the caliber stays high because students will actually have the potential to complete the course.Hence both arguments are strong.

options.text: Neither argument I nor II is strong
options.explanation: Option D is incorrect because both arguments have their strengths. Argument I emphasizes the importance of merit-based admissions for academic excellence, while Argument II highlights the need for inclusivity and addressing social and economic disparities. Both perspectives are valid and need to be balanced in devising an effective admission policy.

questions.explanation: Our country seeks to support educationally and economically backward classes for their overall growth and development.However due to merit based admissions we can assure the caliber stays high because students will actually have the potential to complete the course.Hence both arguments are strong.

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Hire and fire policy' implies 'taking up the performing employees and discarding the non-performing ones'. Clearly, such a policy would stand out to encourage employees to work hard and devotedly to retain their jobs and thus enhance productivity and profitability of the organizations. Argument II seems to be vague in the light of this.

This option is incorrect because both arguments have valid points, and it is not evident that one argument is significantly stronger than the other. Argument II raises concerns about potential management biases in implementing the policy.

options.text: Both arguments I and II are strong
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because while both arguments have some merit, they also have weaknesses. It is not accurate to say that both arguments are equally strong.

options.text: Neither argument I nor II is strong
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because both arguments have reasonable points, even though they also have some flaws. It is not accurate to dismiss both arguments entirely.

questions.explanation: Hire and fire policy' implies 'taking up the performing employees and discarding the non-performing ones'. Clearly, such a policy would stand out to encourage employees to work hard and devotedly to retain their jobs and thus enhance productivity and profitability of the organizations. Argument II seems to be vague in the light of this.

questions.text: How many such pairs of letters are there in the word 'HISTORICAL' which have as many letters between them in the word as they have between them in the English alphabet?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Total Six Pairs: They are: 1) HI 2) ST 3) I L 4) LS 5) CI 6) RT *Note that in these six pairs of words the difference in alphabets in-between the letter 'HISTORICAL' and in English alphabets is the same. For example: The alphabets H and I have zero alphabets in between the letter HISTORICAL and zero in English alphabets (A-Z). Same is the case for Pair No 2: (ST). Similarly Pair 3 (IL) have a difference of 2 alphabets in both the letter HISTORICAL and in English alphabets (A-Z). The other 3 follow similar sequence but in a backwards direction. Means the counting should be made from right to left side.

Total Six Pairs They are: 1) HI 2) ST 3) I L 4) LS 5) CI 6) RT *Note that in these six pairs of words the difference in alphabets in-between the letter 'HISTORICAL' and in English alphabets is the same. Similarly Pair 3 (IL) have a difference of 2 alphabets in both the letter HISTORICAL and in English alphabets (A-Z). The other 3 follow similar sequence but in a backwards direction. Means the counting should be made from right to left side.

Similarly Pair 3 (IL) have a difference of 2 alphabets in both the letter HISTORICAL and in English alphabets (A-Z). The other 3 follow similar sequence but in a backwards direction. Means the counting should be made from right to left side.

Total Six Pairs: They are: 1) HI 2) ST 3) I L 4) LS 5) CI 6) RT *Note that in these six pairs of words the difference in alphabets in-between the letter 'HISTORICAL' and in English alphabets is the same. For example: The alphabets H and I have zero alphabets in between the letter HISTORICAL and zero in English alphabets (A-Z). Same is the case for Pair No 2: (ST). Similarly Pair 3 (IL) have a difference of 2 alphabets in both the letter HISTORICAL and in English alphabets (A-Z). The other 3 follow similar sequence but in a backwards direction. questions.text: Artificial Language: Here are some words translated from an artificial language. Dionot means oak tree, blyonot means oak leaf, blycrin means maple leaf. Which word could mean "maple syrup"?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.explanation: Let's break down the given words: Dionot means oak tree Blyonot means oak leaf Blycrin means maple leaf This suggests that "bly" likely represents the common part for something related to a leaf. Therefore, "bly" probably means "leaf" and ''crin'' means ''maple.'' So, we can assume that ''weel" means syrup. So correct answer would be crinweel.

questions.text: Logical games. Select the odd word from the following:
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: An herb is a type of plant that is valued for its medicinal, culinary, or aromatic properties. Herbs are a distinct category of plants and can include various species like basil, mint, or rosemary.

A flower is a reproductive structure found in many plants. It is typically colorful and is responsible for producing seeds. Flowers are a defining feature of many plant species and play a vital role in pollination.

A tree is a large woody perennial plant with a well-defined trunk, branches, and leaves. Trees are characterized by their tall height and ability to provide shade and support for various organisms. They are a distinct category of plants and can include species such as oak, pine, or maple.

A shrub is a small to medium-sized woody plant with multiple stems and a shorter height compared to trees. Shrubs often have dense foliage and can be found in various habitats. Examples of shrubs include roses, azaleas, and junipers.

questions.explanation: All options except ‘flower’ are different varieties of plants, while ‘flower’ grows on plants. Hence, ‘flower’ is the odd one out.

questions.text: Look at this series: 7, 10,8, 11, 9, 12. Which number do you think will be next?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: A is correct since this is a 3 and 2 series. We are adding 3 to 7 so it becomes 10. Then we subtract 2 from 10 so that it becomes 8. Then we add 3 to 8 so it becomes 11. Then we subtract 2 from 11 so it becomes 9. We add 3 to 9 so it becomes 12. Finally, when we subtract 2 from 12, we get 10.
B is incorrect since it does not give the right answer.
C is incorrect since it does not give the right answer.
D is incorrect since it does not give the right answer.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct since this is a 3 and 2 series. We are adding 3 to 7 so it becomes 10. Then we subtract 2 from 10 so that it becomes 8. Then we add 3 to 8 so it becomes 11. Then we subtract 2 from 11 so it becomes 9. We add 3 to 9 so it becomes 12. Finally, when we subtract 2 from 12, we get 10. Option B is incorrect since it does not give the right answer. Option C is incorrect since it does not give the right answer. Option D is incorrect since it does not give the right answer.

questions.text: A police officer stationed along the route of a parade has been ordered not to allow cars to cross the route while the parade is in progress. An ambulance driver on an emergency run attempts to drive an ambulance across the route while the parade is passing. Under these circumstances, the officer should:
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Ask the driver to wait while the officer calls headquarters and obtain a decision.
Stop the parade long enough to permit the ambulance to cross the street.
options.explanation: Without any knowledge of police rules, common sense dictates that saving lives is the number one priority. An ambulance on an emergency run is on a mission to save a life. Lifesaving takes precedence over the desire for an uninterrupted parade, despite the officer's prior orders.

options.text: Direct the ambulance driver to the shortest detour available, which will add at least ten minutes to the run.
Hold up the ambulance in accordance with the order.

questions.explanation: Without any knowledge of police rules, common sense dictates that saving lives is the number one priority. An ambulance on an emergency run is on a mission to save a life. Lifesaving takes precedence over the desire for an uninterrupted parade, despite the officer’s prior orders.

questions.text: Statement and Argument:Statement 1: The government has decided to roll back the hike in the prices of cooking gas and kerosene. Statement 2: Some ministers had resigned in protest against the hike in prices of cooking gas and other petroleum products. Select the correct option.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: I is the cause and II is effect
options.explanation: Option a) I is the cause, and II is the effect.Explanation: This option suggests that Statement 1 (the government rolling back the price hike) is the cause, and Statement 2 (some ministers resigning in protest) is its effect. In other words, the government's decision to roll back the price hike has led to some ministers resigning in protest.

options.text: II is the cause and I is the effect
options.explanation: Option B is correct , 2 is the cause and 1 is effect.

Option c) Both are independent causes.Explanation: This option suggests that both Statement 1 (the government rolling back the price hike) and Statement 2 (some ministers resigning in protest) are independent causes. It means that these two events are happening separately and are not directly related to each other as cause and effect.

options.text: Both are effects of independent causes
options.explanation: Option d) Both are effects of independent causes.Explanation: This option suggests that both Statement 1 (the government rolling back the price hike) and Statement 2 (some ministers resigning in protest) are effects of independent causes. In other words, there are separate reasons or events that have caused both the government's decision to roll back the price hike and the ministers' resignations.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct, as the reduction in petroleum prices was a result of resignation of ministers.

questions.text: Statement and Argument:Statement Should coal engines be replaced by electric engines in trains?Arguments {I) Yes. Coal engines cause a lot of pollution.{II) Yes. Electric engines are good on performance, easy to operate, and low on maintenance. {Ill) No. Pakistan does not produce enough electricity to fulfill its domestic needs also. Choose the correct option.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.Option a) All are strongExplanation: This option suggests that all three arguments (I, II, and III) are strong and valid points in the context of the statement. In other words, each argument presents a compelling reason to either support or oppose the replacement of coal engines with electric engines in trains.

options.text: Only I and II are strong
options.explanation: Option 1 and 2 are strong arguments.Option b) Only I and II are strongExplanation: This option indicates that arguments I and II are strong and valid points, but argument III is weak. It means that the environmental concerns and performance advantages of electric engines (I and II) are strong reasons to support their adoption in trains, while the argument related to Pakistan's electricity production (III) is not compelling enough to oppose the switch.

options.text: Only II and Ill are strong
options.explanation: This option is incorrect.Option c) Only II and III are strongExplanation: This option suggests that arguments II and III are strong and valid points, but argument I is weak. It means that the performance benefits and electricity production concerns (II and III) are strong reasons either to support or oppose the replacement, while the argument about pollution caused by coal engines (I) is not compelling enough.

options.text: Only I and Ill are strong
options.explanation: This option is incorrect.Option d) Only I and III are strongExplanation: This option indicates that arguments I and III are strong and valid points, but argument II is weak. It means that the environmental concerns and electricity production issues (I and III) are strong reasons either to support or oppose the replacement, while the argument about the performance advantages of electric engines (II) is not compelling enough.

questions.explanation: Should coal engines be replaced by electric engines in trains? Coal engines are responsible for creating a huge amount of pollution in contrary to electric engines.Also, their performance, operation, and maintenance are easy as compared to electric engines. So, arguments I and II seem to be true and III is vague.

questions.text: Statements and Conclusions:Statements Nutritious food is delicious and good for health. Apple is nutritious. Conclusions (I) Apple is good for health. (II) Delicious foods are nutritious.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Apple is nutritious and nutritious food is good for health hence apple is good for health. However not all delicious food is nutritious like chips so statement 2 is incorrect.

b) Only conclusion (II) followsExplanation: Conclusion (II) states that "Delicious foods are nutritious." This conclusion does not directly follow from the given statements. While the first statement does say that nutritious food is delicious, it does not necessarily mean that all delicious foods are nutritious. The relationship between deliciousness and nutritional value is not necessarily one-to-one. Some delicious foods may not be nutritious, and some nutritious foods may not be considered delicious. Therefore, conclusion (II) does not necessarily follow.

c) Both conclusions followExplanation: This option suggests that both conclusions (I) and (II) follow from the given statements. However, as explained earlier, conclusion (II) does not necessarily follow, so this option is not correct.

options.text: Both of them do not follow
options.explanation: d) Both of them do not followExplanation: This option suggests that neither conclusion (I) nor conclusion (II) follows from the given statements. However, as explained earlier, conclusion (I) does follow from the given statements, so this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: Apple is nutritious and nutritious food is good for health hence apple is good for health. However not all delicious food is nutritious like chips so statement 2 is incorrect.

questions.text: Look at this series 80, 10, 70, 15, 60. What number should come next?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: This is an alternating addition and subtraction series. In the first pattern, 10 is subtracted from each number to arrive at the next. In the second, 5 is added to each number to arrive at the next. First pattern (80) (80 - 10 = 70) (70 - 10 = 60) (60 - 10 = 50). Second pattern (10) (10 + 5 = 15) (15 + 5 = 20 So the answer is a = 20.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: This is an alternating addition and subtraction series. In the first pattern, 10 is subtracted from each number to arrive at the next. In the second, 5 is added to each number to arrive at the next. First pattern (80) (80 - 10 = 70) (70 - 10 = 60) (60 - 10 = 50). Second pattern (10) (10 + 5 = 15) (15 + 5 = 20) So the answer is a = 20.

questions.text: Which of the following expressions may not be true if the following expressions are true? (a) Z > Y (b) Y = W (c) W < X
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: As we know Y=W so we can put W in place of Y in equation a, so we will get equation of this option , so its right.
We don't get this relation in any case by shuffling constants.
If we put Y in place of W in equation c , we get equation of this option, as Y=W.
As in equation a, Z is either equal to or greater than Y ,so Z=Y is true.

questions.explanation: Statement : Z ≥ Y = W and X ≥ W = Y (A) : W ≤ Z => True, since Z ≥ W. (B) : X ≥ Z => Not true (C) : Y ≤ X => True Thus, only (B) may not be true....

questions.text: Asiya goes 30 km towards North from a fixed point, then after turning to her right she goes another 15 km. Then she takes another right and goes 30km. How far and in what direction is she from her starting point?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Its better to draw coordinate axis on plane. The first 30 km north and the subsequent 15 km east create a right-angled triangle.
Option B is correct. In reference to the given image her current position is 15km East from the point where she started.
Option C (20 km east) is incorrect because after moving 30 km towards the North and taking two right turns, Asiya's final position is 15 km to the east from her starting point.
Option D (25 km east) is incorrect because her final position is 15 km east, not 25 km.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. In reference to the given image her current position is 15km East from the point where she started.

questions.text: Statement: Life means stresses and strains. Stresses and strains have an adverse effect on life. Conclusion: I. Life is not worth living. II. One should avoid stresses and strains in life.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Both conclusion I and II follows.

questions.explanation: Conclusion I cannot be followed. There is no mention in the statement that life is not worth living. Conclusion II follows, as one should avoid stresses and strains because they have an adverse effect on life. Hence, only conclusion II follows.

questions.text: Statement: The increasing population of our nation will lead to the depletion of many essential resources. Conclusion: I. Population of our nation can be controlled. II. The nation will not be able to provide a decent living to its citizens.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Either conclusion I or II follows.
Both conclusion I and II follows.

questions.explanation: Only conclusion II follows as the nation will not be able to provide a decent living to its citizens because of the increasing population of our nation.

questions.text: Statement: Smoking is one of the human weaknesses, which tends to test the willpower of the smoker to the edge. Conclusion: I. It is very difficult for the smokers to give up smoking even if they want to. II. Human beings have other weaknesses as well.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Either conclusion I or II follows.
Both conclusion I and II follows.

questions.explanation: The statement can clearly mean that smoking is one of the weaknesses of humans which tends to test the willpower of humans at the extreme point. Hence, both the conclusion follows.

questions.text: In the following question, three statements are given followed by four Conclusions. You have to consider the statements to be true even if they seems to be at variance from commonly known facts. You have to decide which of the given Conclusions can definitely be drawn from the given statements. Indicate your answer. Statements: a). In a consignment, all the boxes contain books. b). Some of these boxes have also note-books. c). Some other boxes have also scribbling pads. Conclusions: In this consignment, I. Some boxes contain only scribbling pads. II. All boxes contain notebooks. III. Some boxes contain notebook and books. IV. Some boxes contain notebooks, scribbling pads, and books.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.explanation: All the boxes contain books. Some of these boxes contain both note-books and books. Some of these boxes contain both books and scribbling pads. Therefore, only conclusion III follows. Hence, the correct option is (A).

questions.text: Statements: Company X has a record of manufacturing cameras of quality and the latest design so that you do not spoil even a single shot irrespective of the weather conditions. Conclusions: I. No other company except X is reputed in the camera industry. II. Anyone can take an acceptable shot with camera X.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.explanation: Clearly, the statement talks of Company X only and no other company. So, I does not follow. Also, it is mentioned that one can take a good shot even in bad weather conditions with a camera of Company X. So, II follows.
